name: Seven Against Thebes
shortDescription: Aeschylus' tragedy depicting the epic battle for Thebes, where seven champions attack the seven gates of the city.
description: Seven Against Thebes is a tragedy by Aeschylus, first performed in 467 BC. It forms the final part of a connected trilogy, preceded by *Laius* and *Oedipus*, though these are now lost. The play recounts the siege of Thebes by a Argive army led by Polynices, son of Oedipus, against his brother Eteocles, who refused to yield the throne as agreed. Each of the seven Argive heroes attacks one of the seven gates of Thebes, facing a Theban defender. The play focuses heavily on the anticipation of the battle, the descriptions of the attackers and defenders at each gate, and the grim resolve of Eteocles. Ultimately, the brothers, Polynices and Eteocles, kill each other in single combat, fulfilling the curse placed upon them by their father Oedipus. The play's themes of civic duty, familial conflict, and the power of fate resonate deeply within Greek tragedy.
subtitle: A city besieged, a family divided by fate.
author: Aeschylus
significance: One of the oldest surviving tragedies, exploring themes of war, fate, and civic responsibility.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The story of the Seven Against Thebes is rooted in the Theban cycle of myths, which centers on the tragic fate of Oedipus and his descendants. Oedipus, after unknowingly killing his father and marrying his mother, cursed his sons, Polynices and Eteocles, to divide their inheritance by the sword. After Oedipus's death, Eteocles seized the throne of Thebes and refused to share it with Polynices. Polynices, exiled from Thebes, gathered an army from Argos, including several legendary heroes, to reclaim his rightful share. The play itself focuses on the immediate events of the siege, with messengers reporting the progress of the battle at each of the seven gates.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: In ancient Greece, *Seven Against Thebes* served as a powerful exploration of civic duty and the consequences of familial strife. The play highlights the importance of defending one's city, even in the face of overwhelming odds. Eteocles, though aware of the curse and his impending doom, chooses to fight for Thebes, embodying the ideal of a responsible leader. The play also served as a cautionary tale about the destructive nature of ambition and the futility of war. The chorus, composed of Theban women, expresses the fear and anxiety of the civilian population caught in the conflict, providing a counterpoint to the heroic ideals of the warriors.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The number seven holds symbolic significance in the play, representing completeness and totality. The seven gates of Thebes symbolize the city's vulnerability, while the seven attackers and defenders represent the full spectrum of human courage and folly. The curse of Oedipus looms large, symbolizing the inescapable power of fate and the consequences of past actions. The play can be interpreted as a commentary on the cyclical nature of violence and the destructive potential of unchecked ambition. The fratricidal climax underscores the devastating consequences of internal conflict and the breakdown of familial bonds.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: *Seven Against Thebes* has had a lasting influence on Western literature and art. Its themes of war, fate, and civic duty continue to resonate with modern audiences. The play has been adapted and reinterpreted in various forms, including operas, novels, and films. Its exploration of leadership, sacrifice, and the psychological impact of war remains relevant in contemporary society. The play's iconic imagery, such as the seven gates and the dueling brothers, has inspired countless artists and writers, solidifying its place as a cornerstone of Western culture.

sources.author: Aeschylus
sources.description: The primary source for the myth and the basis for subsequent interpretations.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: Provides background on Oedipus's curse and its impact on his sons.

associations.name: War
associations.significance: Central to the plot and themes of the play.

associations.name: Fate
associations.significance: The inescapable force driving the tragic events.

associations.name: Curse
associations.significance: Oedipus's curse dooms his sons to fratricide.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Mahabharata War
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Features a large-scale battle with heroes on opposing sides and themes of familial conflict and duty.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Siege of Troy
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A protracted siege with heroes battling over a city and themes of war and fate.

companions.name: Oedipus
companions.description: The father of Polynices and Eteocles, whose curse sets the stage for the conflict.

companions.name: Polynices
companions.description: One of the seven leaders attacking Thebes to reclaim his throne.

companions.name: Eteocles
companions.description: King of Thebes who defends the city against his brother's attack.

cultural.modernLegacy: The play continues to be studied and performed, influencing literature, theater, and film. Its themes remain relevant in contemporary discussions of war, leadership, and family dynamics.
